sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
if last_transition:
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation['nodes']
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation['nodes']
    before_colors = {n['color']: n['pixels'] for n in before}
    after_colors = {n['color']: n['pixels'] for n in after}
    print(f"Last action: {last_transition.action}")
    for c in sorted(set(list(before_colors.keys()) + list(after_colors.keys()))):
        bc = before_colors.get(c, 0)
        ac = after_colors.get(c, 0)
        if bc != ac:
            print(f"  {c}: {bc} -> {ac} ({ac-bc:+d})")

# Check magenta cursor positions now
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M':
        print(f"M cursor: id={n['id']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking (14, 28) increased W from 6 to 31 (+25). So the pattern alternates:
- Click (46, 52) → W decreases significantly (33→6, -27)
- Click (14, 28) → W increases (6→31, +25)
So clicking (46, 52) reduces W, clicking (14, 28) increases it. The goal is to reduce W to 0. I should keep clicking (46, 52) repeatedly instead of alternating. But wait, the game might require alternating. Let me think...
Actually, looking at the pattern more carefully:
- When I click (46, 52), W goes down (good)
- When I click (14, 28), W goes up (bad)
So the efficient approach is to just keep clicking (46, 52). But the game seems to have this alternating mechanic. Let me try clicking (46, 52) again to see if it still reduces W.

<parameter=code>

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 52}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
